title: Why polyvagal theory changed everything about trauma treatment
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
The moment I stopped blaming myself

I spent years thinking I was broken. Not in a dramatic, self-pitying way. Just a quiet, persistent knowing that something inside me didn't work right. I'd read self-help books, tried meditation apps, gone to therapy. And still, my body would hijack me. I'd be in a meeting, perfectly fine, and suddenly my heart would race. My throat would close. My hands would go cold. I'd excuse myself to the bathroom and stare at my face in the mirror, thinking: What is wrong with you?

Here's the thing. Nothing was wrong with me. I just didn't understand my nervous system.

Then I found polyvagal theory. Dr. Stephen Porges, a neuroscientist who's been studying the vagus nerve for decades, published the foundational work in 1994. But it took another decade or so for clinicians to really start integrating it into trauma treatment. And when they did, everything shifted. Not just for therapists. For people like me. People who'd been told they were "too sensitive" or "overreacting" or "dramatic."

Polyvagal theory gave me a map. Not a cure. Not a quick fix. A map. And maps don't fix the terrain. They just show you where you are, where the cliffs are, and where the safe ground might be. That alone changed everything.

Think about it. Before polyvagal theory, most trauma treatment focused on the story. What happened to you. The narrative. The memory. And that's important work. Bessel van der Kolk, who wrote The Body Keeps the Score, spent decades showing us that trauma lives in the body, not just the mind. But even he'd tell you that knowing the story isn't enough. You have to address the nervous system's response. The part that's still scanning for danger, even when you're safe.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice (paid link). Peter Levine's work on somatic experiencing pairs beautifully with polyvagal theory. Together, they offer a way to work with the body's wisdom, not against it.

The three states you didn't know you were in

Polyvagal theory says we have three primary nervous system states. Not two. Three. For decades, we talked about fight-or-flight (sympathetic activation) and rest-and-digest (parasympathetic activation). But Porges identified a third state: the dorsal vagal state. This is the freeze response. The collapse. The shutdown. The part of you that goes numb when things get too overwhelming.

Here's what that looks like in real life. You're in an argument with your partner. Your voice gets tight. Your muscles tense. That's sympathetic activation. Fight-or-flight. You're ready to defend yourself or run. But then something else happens. You go silent. Your thoughts go blank. You feel heavy, almost sleepy. That's dorsal vagal. Freeze. Collapse. Your system decided the threat was too big, so it shut down to protect you.

Wild, right? Your body has a built-in emergency brake. And it's not your fault when it slams on. It's a survival response that kept your ancestors alive. But in modern life, it can make you feel crazy.

Janina Fisher, a trauma specialist who trained with van der Kolk, talks about how these states show up in therapy. She says clients often present as "difficult" or "resistant" when really their nervous system is just stuck in a protective state. They're not being difficult. They're being terrified. Or numb. Or both. And the therapist's job isn't to push through that. It's to help the system find its way back to safety.

I remember the first time I understood this. I was sitting in my car after a particularly rough therapy session. My therapist had asked me about something from my childhood, and I'd gone completely blank. I couldn't speak. Couldn't think. I felt like I was underwater. Later, she explained that my dorsal vagal state had activated. My body had gone into freeze because the memory was too much. And I realized: I'd been doing this my whole life. Disappearing when things got hard. Not because I was weak. Because my nervous system was trying to protect me.

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger (paid link). Peter Levine walks through how animals in the wild release trauma through physical shaking and movement. Humans do it too, but we've been taught to suppress it. The book offers practical ways to let that natural release happen.

Why talk therapy wasn't enough

I'm not here to trash talk therapy. It saved my life, honestly. But there was a limit to what I could access through words alone. I could talk about my childhood for hours. I could analyze my patterns, understand my attachment style, make sense of my parents' failures. And still, my body would react to a raised voice or a sudden noise like I was being attacked.

That's because the vagus nerve doesn't speak English. It speaks sensation. Pressure. Temperature. Vibration. Rhythm. Tone of voice. Eye contact. These are the languages your nervous system actually listens to. And talk therapy, as helpful as it is, often stays in the verbal .

Dan Siegel, who coined the term "interpersonal neurobiology," talks about how the brain and body are shaped by relationships. Your nervous system learns what safety feels like through your earliest attachments. If those attachments were inconsistent or threatening, your system learns to expect danger. It becomes hypervigilant. Or it learns to collapse into shutdown. Either way, it's not a choice. It's a survival adaptation.

So what actually helps? Polyvagal-informed approaches focus on something called "neuroception." That's Porges' term for how your nervous system scans for cues of safety or danger, below your conscious awareness. You can't decide to feel safe. Your nervous system decides. But you can give it better data. You can create conditions that help it shift toward the ventral vagal state, which is the social engagement state. The state where you feel connected, calm, and present.

How do you do that? Slowly. Gently. With practices that signal safety to the body. Things like humming, which stimulates the vagus nerve. Slow, deep exhales. Soft eye contact with someone you trust. Rocking or swaying. Warmth on your skin. These aren't woo-woo fixes. They're physiological interventions. They work because your vagus nerve responds to them.

Something practical for those looking for gentle nervous system support is NOW L-Theanine 200mg (paid link). L-theanine is an amino acid found in green tea that promotes relaxation without drowsiness. It helps take the edge off without numbing you. I use it on days when my system feels revved up and I need a little help settling down.

The shame spiral and how to stop it

Here's the hardest part. When your nervous system flips into fight-or-flight or freeze, your brain doesn't just react physically. It also generates a story. A narrative about what's happening and why. And that story is almost always shame-based. "I'm overreacting." "I'm broken." "Everyone else can handle this, why can't I?" "Something is wrong with me."

That shame makes everything worse. It activates your nervous system further, creating a feedback loop. You feel anxious, so you shame yourself for feeling anxious. The shame triggers more anxiety. Your system gets more dysregulated. And you end up stuck, feeling even more broken than when you started.

Gabor Mate, who works with trauma and addiction, talks about this. He says that trauma isn't what happens to you. It's what happens inside you as a result of what happens to you. The shame, the disconnection, the loss of trust in your own body. That's the real wound. And it doesn't heal through force. It heals through presence.

What does presence look like in practice? It looks like noticing. "Oh, my heart is racing. That's my sympathetic system activating." Not "I'm having a heart attack." Not "I'm dying." Just noticing. "Oh, my hands are cold. That's blood moving to my core, preparing for danger." Not "Something terrible is about to happen." Just a fact. A data point.

Deb Dana, who wrote extensively on applying polyvagal theory in clinical practice, calls this "glimmering." Glimmers are the opposite of triggers. They're small moments of safety. A warm cup of coffee. A bird outside your window. A friend's voice on the phone. Your job isn't to eliminate triggers. It's to notice glimmers. To let your system collect evidence that the world is safe enough.

I started doing this. I'd walk through my apartment and find glimmers. The soft blanket on my couch. The smell of my tea. The way the afternoon light fell on my bookshelf. And over time, my nervous system started to trust. Not completely. Not perfectly. But enough. Enough that the shame spiral lost some of its power.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). Van der Kolk's work is dense, but it's also deeply validating. He lays out the science in a way that makes you feel seen. Like someone finally understands what you've been living with.

Where we go from here

I'm not going to tell you that polyvagal theory fixed me. It didn't. I still have days where my nervous system goes haywire. I still get triggered by things that don't make logical sense. I still feel shame sometimes when I react in ways I don't understand.

But here's what changed. I stopped believing the shame. I stopped thinking I was broken. I started seeing my reactions as protective strategies, not character flaws. And that shift, as small as it sounds, opened up space. Space to breathe. Space to experiment. Space to try new things without the pressure of needing to be "fixed."

Polyvagal theory didn't give me a cure. It gave me a framework. A way to understand what was happening in my body without judging it. And for me, that was enough to start healing.

If you're reading this and recognizing yourself in any of it, I want you to know something. Your nervous system isn't your enemy. It's not trying to sabotage you. It's trying to protect you, the only way it knows how. And with time, patience, and the right support, it can learn new ways. It can learn that you're safe now. That you don't need to stay on high alert. That you can rest.

It takes time. It's not linear. Some days you'll feel like you're back at square one. That's normal. That's the process. Stay with it. Stay with yourself. Your system is listening.

Healing Journey Resources

  • In an Unspoken Voice (paid link) - Peter Levine's exploration of body-based healing and trauma release.
  • Waking the Tiger (paid link) - A somatic approach to healing trauma through natural physical responses.
  • The Body Keeps the Score (paid link) - Bessel van der Kolk's look at how trauma affects the brain and body.
  • NOW L-Theanine 200mg (paid link) - A gentle supplement for supporting nervous system calm and focus.
